abstract: Precise adjustment of metal-organic framework (MOF) architectures at sub-angstrom scales is a powerful method for enhancing gas separation, yet its impact often diminishes in materials with larger pores. Here, we present an integrative design strategy that amplifies the effects of ultrafine pore tuning by first inducing extreme pore compression. This approach utilizes the multi-modular partitioned acs (pacs) platform, which exhibits high tolerance for isoreticular substitution. By employing a bioisosteric replacement strategy—substituting the standard 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate with trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate (tcb)—we achieved a massive 30% reduction in the unit-cell c/a ratio. This "extreme compression" established a confined environment where subsequent ultrafine tuning (less than 3% variation) via a second ligand module became highly influential. Consequently, the C2H2/CO2 selectivity increased dramatically from 2.6 to 20.8, a performance confirmed by experimental gas breakthrough studies. This work demonstrates that the use of small, cyclic dicarboxylates like tcb provides a significant new route for sub-angstrom engineering and high-efficiency gas purification.
